sections.about: This metric measures total water discharged after use and treatment and is disclosed under BRSR Principle 6 Essential Indicator water discharge by destination and level of treatment. Lower discharge generally indicates higher recycling/reuse and movement toward ZLD, provided consumption is not simply increasing through evaporative losses. A lower value indicates better performance.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: JSW and Jindal report zero discharge in FY2024 and FY2025, making them leaders on ZLD-style reported performance. Tata reports 12.00 million kL in FY2023, rising to 14.01 million kL in FY2024 before improving to 12.22 million kL in FY2025, ranking 3rd in all three years. SAIL worsens from 22.99 million kL to 41.81 million kL, despite reporting ZLD initiatives and treatment/recirculation of approximately 128 million m³/year.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2025, Tata ranks 3rd. JSW and Jindal are best at zero; percentage gap is not meaningful, and Tata's absolute gap is 12.22 million kL. Tata ranks 3rd across all three years; the FY2023-24 trend worsened from 12.00 million kL to 14.01 million kL before improving to 12.22 million kL in FY2025.
sections.targets: Tata aims for ZED from Works premises in India and uses CETP, reverse osmosis, STPs and internal reuse for coke quenching, BF slag granulation, steel slag quenching, sinter/pellet mixing, GCP, horticulture and dust suppression. SAIL states all upcoming units are envisaged on ZLD concept.
sections.comparabilityNotes: Zero discharge should be validated with treatment and reuse boundary definitions. Very low discharge can coexist with high consumption where evaporation/cooling losses are high. Tata Steel FY2022-23 water discharge metrics were not explicitly reported on a purely standalone basis but rather collectively for Tata Steel India and its Indian subsidiaries.
